17 min read
0:00 / 17:16
Our objective for this module is to give you a framework to create
a succinct, compelling, and persuasive description of the value
you can create for a minimum viable audience as it relates to the
jobs they want to accomplish, pains they want to avoid, and/or
gains they want to acquire.
To do that, we’re going to borrow some ideas from our friends at
Sterling & Stone, and introduce you to a TDB-speci�c ‘writing
marathon’ inspired by one of our favorite books of all time —
Accidental Genius.
Since October 3, 2011, Sterling & Stone co-founders (and writing
partners) Sean Platt, Johnny Truant, and David Wright have
published more than 100 books and written more than six million
words.
They’ve also written more than 1.5 million words in a single year.
(To put that into perspective, the entire Harry Potter series is
1,084,170 words.)
Sean, Johnny, and David have developed a step-by-step ‘Story
Beats’ process that we’ll distill and modify (slightly) for our
purposes. They’re writing �ction (upwards of 100K words per
Validate Part II (Messaging &
Writing Framework)
MP3 File 5.93 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 15 --

book) — we’re writing much sorter non-�ction emails, marketing
messages, landing pages, ads, and offers.
While there are plenty of differences between the two, the
underlying concepts for writing ‘story beats’ applies to both their
work and ours.
(If you’d like to learn more about their method in its entirety —
especially if you write �ction — we highly recommend their
Udemy Course How to Plan and Outline Novels Using Scrivener.
One caveat — you don’t need to use Scrivener to get a lot of value
from the course. We don’t.)
‘Story beats’ are, in Sean’s words, sketches, signposts, and seeds of
ideas you, as the writer, can expand on:
“Beats are not gospel … their job is to get you going through the draft.”
In simpler terms, think about beats as an outline of ideas. The
goal initially is to get those ideas out of your head and onto a page
without getting wrapped up in concerns about writing. Beats
aren’t prose — they’re succinct descriptions of ideas.
An aside by André: Making Magic
(READING THIS IS NOT REQUIRED FOR THIS LESSON.)
This won’t resonate with everyone, but if it resonates with you, the
experience can feel like making magic (or like you’ve been sent a gift from
the Gods).
In the book Writing As a Way of Being, Robert Yagelski explains the
di�erence between self-expression (through writing) and expression of
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 15 --

the self (through writing).
Writing is a form of self-expression. You have a self separate from the
writing process; you have thoughts in your head, and writing is a tool to
bring those thoughts to an audience. This is the form of writing we all
know about.
Expression of the self (through writing) is when the self exists in the
process of writing; it’s a becoming that happens in the physical process
of writing that CREATES THOUGHT ITSELF at that moment.
When you read it like that, it’s hard to make sense of what that feels like or
what it even means.
Intellectually, yup, it makes sense … but you’ll likely still be left with: but
yeah, I still don’t really ‘get it.’
… until you experience it and literally see the magic happen in front of
your eyes. Then it’s like … oh d#ck, what just happened?!
I was gifted with some of the magic earlier this week. When “the Muse”
shows up like this, it’s hard to explain the feeling of awe.
Case in point: I’ve been working on a (pleasure) project with my writing
coach, Alida, the past few weeks. I’m writing a novel (or novella). It’s urban
fantasy, which seemed like a fun idea at the time.
I had an idea of the protagonist, our reluctant hero. But the antagonist,
his/her motivation, the plot — all of the import elements of a story — was a
complete mystery to me.
So Alida had me freewriting; write any scene about the protagonist, which
I did, no issues there. It was fun; some details emerged that I didn’t know
about.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 15 --

Then Alida, the wily old fox, said I should do the same for the antagonist. I
could see what she was up to, so I gave it a shot, but other than some
elements I already had an idea about, nothing ‘magical’ revealed itself. I
still didn’t know anything new.
Last Friday, we talked it through a bit more, and she said, “I want you to
write the opening scene to your story.”
Fantastic, I thought. Not!
We deconstructed the opening scene to Orphan Black on YouTube. It’s a 3
minute scene that is full of unanswered questions.
“Do that,” she said. Use that as inspiration.
Right, right … easy, no problem.
The writing lessons we’ll unpack in this lesson are essentially what I did
this week for the writing I’m doing for Alida.
�. 6.5 mins �ow writing breathing exercise (25 deep breaths),
�. 30-minute timer (I use a 30-minute sand timer pictured below),
�. Write! (no stopping)
Note: Our friend, Mike Rinard, has kindly allowed us to share his ‘�ow
writing’ breathing exercise (linked above) with our LBC/TDB students.
(Nice one, Mike!)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 15 --

I literally had no idea what I was doing to write…
I wrote a scene on Monday in the time:
The sun was almost set as a purple hue enveloped the warm city. People
were out, drawn to the connections of social circles in sweaty bars, or
were being expressed back onto the streets in noisy laughter to �nd the
next watering hole…
… then, in the moment of writing the above exposition, a character
revealed himself to me, and I pulled the thread. First, I thought it was my
antagonist, so I continued to write until the sands of time ran out.
At this point, I presumed it was my antagonist, but I didn’t have any
details yet.
Tuesday morning, I opened a new page, and after the breathing, �ipped
the sandglass over and wrote the start of a new scene:
Marty entered the lobby of an all-glass building in a district of the city
known for the highest concentration of ‘bulldog’ law �rms. If you
needed a lawyer, this was not the place to seek council; unless ‘you’
meant nine or ten-�gure corporation. Marty was feeling a little delicate
from the night before…
… and so it unfolded. After thirty minutes, I stopped. I have no idea who
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 15 --

When Sean, Johnny, and David write stories they rely on four
documents that inform their writing. Those documents are:
▪ A single sentence description of the book.
▪ Elevator pitch (a 1-2 paragraph description of the book).
▪ Rough story arc (a summary of each stage of the book).
▪ Simple synopsis (“Beats on speed.” Everything in the story
Marty is, the name just showed up as I started to write.
Wednesday morning, same process, new scene:
The two friends found a table at the back of a noisy Starbucks. “Jen, I
don’t know what to do–something weird has just happened,” she said,
her blue eyes wide as they darted left and right. She wore a hazelnut
milk mustache. Curly blond bangs hung wildly. She brushed a stray
curl behind her ear, took another swig of her grande hazelnut
cappuccino. She continued…
… and as the sand was nearing the end of my session, at that moment,
another insight revealed itself, which I hadn’t seen (or even considered).
This week I experienced three moments of expression of the self through
long-hand freewriting. But the really cool thing — kinda like in the movie,
Inception — each new scene revealed an interconnected puzzle piece of the
full story, as I work to assemble it in real-time through my writing.
This sh#t works, folks.
Do the work and be open to what will happen.
Back to the lesson…
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 15 --

told in a rat-a-tat-tat, this happened then this happened
format).
For our purposes, we’re going to use three of those documents,
modi�ed slightly, and add one of our own:
▪ The Hook: a single sentence that succinctly and
persuasively combines the audience you serve with the
value you create.
▪ The Frame: a 300 — 500 work summary that explains who
you serve, the value you create, and why that value is so
important to your audience.
▪ The Manifesto — 1,000 — 2,000 words that dimensionalizes
the value you create in functional, social, and emotional
terms.(Manifestos can be public-facing, if that �ts your
needs, or it can be an internal reference document that
informs your marketing messaging.)
▪ The Dossier — your virtual ‘shoebox’ of ideas captured in a
single document. These can be quotes, stories, beliefs,
evidence, relevant facts, etc. A Dossier collects your story
beats into one ‘staging area’ to draw ideas from.
It’s important to mention that you’re going to create these
documents in reverse order — from broad (dossier) to narrow
(single sentence hook).
Sean Platt explains it this way:
“Rather than build outward from a simple idea, you start by getting
your thoughts onto a page and then making that increasingly
precise.”
Easy, right?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 15 --

We know what you’re thinking — sounds great guys, but how am
I supposed to actually DO that work?
That’s where a ‘writing marathon’ will help.
Our TDB writing marathon is inspired by Accidental Genius. (We
can’t recommend that book enough.) The subtitle of that book is
“Using Writing to Generate Your Best Ideas, Insight, and
Content”, and that’s exactly what we’re going to do.
Here are the steps…
You don’t have to follow these steps exactly, but we encourage you
to try this version, as written, before making any adjustments.
Once you’ve done that you’ll be in a better position to make any
tweaks speci�c to your individual needs (or not).
Here’s what you’ll need for this exercise:
▪ Pen(s) that are easy to write with. No fountain pens, pencils,
mechanical pencils, or anything that will interfere with the
writing process for seven minutes, non-stop.
▪ Paper. This is an analog exercise, not digital. Trust us.
▪ Highlighter.
▪ Timer (your phone alarms and electronic assistants like
Alexa, Google Assistant, and Siri are �ne, or just Google
timer ).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 15 --

The writing process:
▪ Start writing the moment the timer starts and do not stop
until the alarm sounds.
▪ Do not look at the clock, check the time, daydream, eat a
snack, take a sip of coffee (or tea), or even pause when you’re
writing.
▪ Your pen never leaves the page. If you can’t think about
anything to write then write “I can’t think of anything to
write” until you can (or: “Shawn and André are idiots, I want
to punch them in the face … Shawn and André are idiots, I
want to punch them in the face”).
▪ Write whatever comes to mind without any concerns about
formatting, grammar, proper punctuation, etc.
▪ No editing — just write, write, write. Non-stop.
You’re going to write in response to seven prompts. The �rst
session will be seven minutes. All other sessions will be �ve
minutes. You will write all seven sessions in one sitting — no
breaks. (That’s why it’s called a marathon…)
Prompt #1 (7 minutes): “If you are (insert description of your MVA),
here’s how I can help you…”
▪ Accomplish something…
▪ Avoid problems…
▪ Get what you want…
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 15 --

(Write that prompt, and whichever of the three options apply to
your speci�c idea, at the top of a blank sheet of paper, start your
timer, and write continuously for seven minutes without taking
your pen off the paper. No editing.)
For example:
“If you are interested in personal knowledge management, here’s how I
can help you generate ideas high-quality ideas … without locking
yourself in a room for weeks at a time … quickly and dependably
(creativity on demand!)…”
Immediately after you’ve �nished writing for seven minutes, read
what you wrote and highlight anything that stands out for you as
interesting, valuable, persuasive, or well-written.
In particular, you’re looking for references to anything
meaningful you’ve identi�ed that you can help your MVA
accomplish, avoid, and/or acquire.
Prompt #2 (5 minutes): “I can help you accomplish X by…”
Be speci�c. For example, if you identi�ed “learning conversational
Italian” as something meaningful your MVA wants to accomplish
in your �rst seven-minute writing session, prompt #2 might be “I
can help you learn conversational Italian by…”
Then write whatever comes to mind, non-stop, no editing.
Give yourself a 3-5 minute break after each round of writing, then
move on to the next.
Prompt #3 (5 minutes): “I can help you avoid X by…” (X is a pain or
pains you identi�ed in the �rst seven-minute session.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 15 --

For example, if “leaning conversation Italian” is what you’re
helping an audience accomplish, your prompt would be
something like “I can help you avoid spending hours per day
practicing Italian grammar…”
That’ll get you started — then just go with whatever appears after
that. Don’t edit or try to direct where your thoughts go. Let your
thoughts reveal themselves.
Prompt #4 (5 minutes): “I can help you acquire X by…” (X is a gain
you identi�ed in the �rst seven-minute session).
For example: “I can help you acquire conversational Italian
�uency by…” Again, don’t direct or edit. Capture whatever arises.
Prompts 5, 6, and 7 (5 minutes each):
▪ “What you (the audience) want to accomplish is important
because…”
▪ “What you want to avoid is important because…”
▪ “What you want to acquire is important because…”
At the end of this process you will likely feel exhausted. If you
followed the outline above, you will have written non-stop for
thirty-seven minutes, bypassing (or at least undermining) you
inner critic and inner editor, tapping into your subconscious along
the way.
You should have a stack of paper with a lot of writing. Most of that
writing will be junk. However, you’ll likely �nd the seeds of of
some really good ideas.
Those seeds are the raw material for your beats.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 15 --

Grab your highlighter and review what you’ve written. Highlight
anything you notice that is well-written, persuasive, impactful,
important, or meaningful.
After you’ve done that, capture all of those ideas in a digital
Dossier. This can be a simple document, or a more complex
outlining tool like Work�owy, Dynalist, or Roam. Don’t worry
about organizing or editing yet. Just get the full list of ideas into a
single location.
Prompts 1 — 4 identi�ed the what and how of your initial idea to
validate.
Prompts 5 — 7 identi�ed the why.
Combined, you should have a very good start on the ingredients
for a Manifesto. Take some time to polish the ‘beats’ in your
Dossier so they’re clear and concise.
Next, you’ll assemble those beats into a coherent Manifesto. (You
don’t need to use all of them, and you can add ideas that emerge
too.)
Our Manifesto Prompt Questions:
▪ What is the big idea (attention)?
▪ What is the thesis statement (controlling idea)?
▪ What is the context (authority, empathy, evidence, and
explanation to shape beliefs)?
▪ What is an insight I want the reader to discover without
having to explicitly spell it out (Unifying Theory of 2 + 2)?
▪ Conclude (comes full circle) and end with a call to action
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 15 --

(what is the call to action I want the reader to make?).
Keep re�ning that Manifesto until it reads well, sounds right
(when read aloud), and explains the idea you want to validate with
precision and clarity.
Then you’re going to move on to the Frame. Strip the Manifesto
down to the bare elements.
▪ Who do you help?
▪ How do you help them?
▪ Why is that meaningful to them?
Finally, distill your Frame to a single, powerful Hook — 1-2
sentences (one is better). There are lots of formulas for this — an
easy one to use is:
“I help my (audience) achieve (their desire) without (common
problem).”
For example, our one sentence description for TLB might be:
“We help creators make money online without feeling like used car
salesmen.”
One �nal note about the process — give yourself some time for
this exercise (but not too much time).
We recommend a four-step approach:
▪ Step 1: identify the seeds of our idea, in broad strokes, after
completing the work in the Discover Process and Validate,
Part I. You don’t need to know the details, just make sure you
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 15 --

have some general direction.
▪ Step 2: complete the TDB writing marathon, start to �nish,
during your peak creative time. (We’re at our creative best in
the morning and that’s when we do this work. If you’re a
night owl, or your best creative time is in the afternoon, plan
accordingly.)
▪ Step 3: take a mental break for at least 24 — 48 hours. You
may be tempted to go directly from the writing marathon to
creating your simple synopsis. We think you’ll get better
results if you let the ideas you identi�ed in the writing
marathon percolate for a day or two.
▪ Step 4: compile your Dossier �rst, then write a draft of your
Manifesto. Polish that over hours (or maybe even days).
Then re�ne the Manifesto into a Frame, and distill the
Frame to a Hook. This may take a few days to get it right.
We recommend that you limit yourself to three days (or
less). That seems to be the creative Goldilocks sweet spot for
this work.
Once you have these four documents you’ll be ready to share your
ideas with potential prospects to get their feedback.
We’ll explain how to do that next, in Validate, Part III.
Questions or feedback — please use the comments below. Use the
private accountability community to have discussions with your
peers and share your insights with them.
We (strongly) suggest, once you’ve done the exercises in Part II,
that you share your work within the community with your peers.
Finally: enjoy the process, lean into it, be open to what will
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 15 --

emerge.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 15 --
